did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning framework combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs) for multi-class skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset. It also employs Explainable AI (XAI) techniques such as Grad-CAM and occlusion sensitivity to evaluate model interpretability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a hybrid CNN-Vision Transformer framework for skin lesion classification. It details specific methods (dataset balancing, model architecture), results (test accuracy of 45.66%, AUC values), and analysis (Grad-CAM, occlusion sensitivity) on the HAM10000 dataset. Although published in conference proceedings (ICVADV-2026), it reports new empirical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Hybrid CNN–Vision Transformer Framework for Balanced Multi-Class Skin Lesion Classification using the HAM10000 Dataset
journal: Proceedings of the International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV-2026)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of class imbalance in multi-class skin lesion classification by developing a hybrid deep learning framework that combines Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs). The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset, creating a balanced subset of 2,100 dermoscopic images with 300 samples per class across seven diagnostic categories. The model was evaluated using a 70:15:15 train-validation-test split, achieving a test accuracy of 45.66% and high AUC values for specific classes such as VASC (0.94), NV (0.85), and BCC (0.82). Explainable AI techniques, including Grad-CAM and occlusion sensitivity, were employed to verify that the model focused on clinically relevant lesion regions rather than background artifacts. The findings indicate that while overall accuracy is moderate due to inter-class similarity, the hybrid architecture and dataset balancing significantly enhance fairness, interpretability, and discriminative performance for minority classes.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven diagnostic categories in the balanced HAM10000 subset used for multi-class classification. Achieved highest precision (0.71) and AUC of 0.85.
One of the seven diagnostic categories in the balanced HAM10000 subset. Achieved highest recall (0.73) and AUC of 0.80.
One of the seven diagnostic categories in the balanced HAM10000 subset. Subject to confusion with NV and MEL.
One of the seven diagnostic categories in the balanced HAM10000 subset. Achieved AUC of 0.82.
One of the seven diagnostic categories in the balanced HAM10000 subset. Support count of 45 in test set.

One of the seven diagnostic categories in the balanced HAM10000 subset. Minority class in original HAM10000. Achieved highest AUC (0.94) and high precision (0.58).

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin lesions (NV, MEL, BKL, BCC, AKIEC, DF, VASC)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into seven specific skin lesion classes using a hybrid CNN-Vision Transformer framework. The model uses a softmax layer to assign input images to one of the seven categories. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The study utilized a balanced subset created from this dataset by selecting 300 images per class.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid CNN-Vision Transformer model after balancing via controlled undersampling of majority classes and augmentation of minority classes.
use.validation: Used for validation during model optimization (15% of the balanced dataset).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance (15% of the balanced dataset).
use.other: The original HAM10000 dataset is noted to have severe class imbalance, which motivated the creation of the balanced subset used in this study.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the discriminative ability and performance of the model across different classes, particularly for minority classes.
To visualize and analyze misclassifications among visually similar classes (e.g., NV, BKL, MEL) and assess class-wise performance.
To assess the model's fairness and discriminative performance for each of the seven diagnostic categories.
To verify that the model focuses on clinically relevant lesion regions rather than background artifacts, thereby assessing model interpretability and trustworthiness.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC.

HAM10000 (Balanced Subset).notes: The model achieved moderate overall accuracy (45.66%) due to inter-class similarity among NV, BKL, and MEL. However, it demonstrated strong discriminative ability with high AUC values for minority classes like VASC (0.94). Dermatofibroma (DF) showed limited recognition performance (F1 0.00) due to visual subtlety and low support (18 samples). Explainable AI (Grad-CAM and occlusion sensitivity) confirmed the model focused on clinically relevant lesion regions rather than background artifacts.

architecture_details.cnn_features: Fine-grained local texture patterns, pigment networks, edge transitions, border sharpness, and fine textural variations via depthwise separable convolutions and compound scaling
architecture_details.vit_features: Global contextual relationships, long-range spatial dependencies, lesion symmetry, structural coherence, and spatial organization via self-attention mechanisms
architecture_details.fusion_method: Concatenation of flattened feature representations from CNN and ViT branches
architecture_details.post_fusion_layers: Series of fully connected layers with nonlinear activations and dropout regularization
architecture_details.patch_processing: Input image partitioned into fixed-size patches, transformed into embedding vectors via linear projection, with positional encodings added

classification_or_output_head.output_description: Assigns input image to one of seven skin lesion categories

preprocessing_techniques: Dataset balancing via controlled undersampling of majority classes
Targeted augmentation of minority categories including rotation, horizontal and vertical flipping, and contrast adjustments

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to minority categories to supplement the balanced dataset. Specific techniques included rotation, horizontal and vertical flipping, and contrast adjustments.
Used for majority classes to create a balanced dataset of 300 images per class from the HAM10000 dataset.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid CNN-Vision Transformer framework, trained on a balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset (300 images per class), achieved a test accuracy of 45.66%.

Dataset balancing was crucial for preventing majority-class dominance and allowing for a realistic assessment of generalization capability, particularly benefiting minority classes like vascular lesions (VASC) and dermatofibroma (DF).

The model demonstrated strong discriminative ability with high ROC-AUC values for several classes, notably VASC (0.94), NV (0.85), BCC (0.82), DF (0.81), and MEL (0.80), indicating effective separation of positive and negative samples despite moderate overall accuracy.

The hybrid architecture effectively combined CNNs for capturing fine-grained local features (e.g., pigment networks, border irregularities) and Vision Transformers for modeling global contextual relationships (e.g., symmetry, shape), providing richer representations than either component alone.

Explainable AI techniques (Grad-CAM and occlusion sensitivity) confirmed that the model focuses on clinically relevant lesion regions (such as pigment clusters and asymmetries) rather than background artifacts, aligning with dermatological reasoning and enhancing trustworthiness.

High inter-class similarity among visually similar categories (NV, BKL, and MEL) resulted in significant confusion and limited the overall accuracy, reflecting the inherent diagnostic difficulty of these classes.
Dermatofibroma (DF) showed limited recognition performance (F1-score 0.00) due to its inherent visual subtlety and limited support in the test set, despite achieving a competitive AUC of 0.81.

The study indicates that combining hybrid feature extraction with dataset balancing substantially enhances fairness, interpretability, and class-wise learning effectiveness in automated skin cancer diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study uses a heavily manipulated, balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset (300 images per class) rather than the original imbalanced distribution, which limits the generalizability of the results to real-world clinical scenarios where class imbalance is prevalent.
The overall test accuracy is low (45.66%), reflecting the inherent difficulty of distinguishing between visually similar classes (NV, BKL, MEL) even with a balanced dataset.

The model failed completely to recognize Dermatofibroma (DF) in the test set, achieving an F1-score of 0.00, despite having a support count of 18 samples, indicating a critical failure for this specific minority class.

The test set support is very small (total 265-315 samples depending on the specific metric table cited), with some classes having only 18-22 samples, making performance metrics statistically unstable and prone to high variance.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on a split of the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about its ability to generalize to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance tests (p-values) for the performance differences between classes or against baselines.

The hybrid architecture combines EfficientNetB0 and ViT, but the specific fusion mechanism is limited to concatenation of flattened features followed by fully connected layers, which may not optimally integrate local and global features.
While XAI (Grad-CAM and occlusion sensitivity) confirms the model focuses on lesion regions, this is a qualitative assessment and does not guarantee clinical correctness or safety for deployment.

The dataset balancing was achieved via controlled undersampling of majority classes and targeted augmentation of minority classes, which may introduce artificial patterns or reduce the diversity of the majority class representations.
